ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to ranch dressing exists; A0CKW (Salad dressing) is the closest defensible parent category match. Ranch dressing is a mayonnaise-and-buttermilk-based emulsified salad dressing that falls within this broader FoodEx2 category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ranch dressing as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ranch dressing is approximately 2 tablespoons (30 g), used as a dip for vegetables, wings, or pizza, or as a salad dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Ranch dressing, regular — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172942)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record values (energy 430 kcal, protein 1.32 g, fat 44.54 g, carbohydrate 5.9 g, sugar 4.69 g, sodium 810 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, potassium 64 mg, calcium 28 mg, magnesium 5 mg, iron 0.3 mg, cholesterol 26 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for regular ranch dressing and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at 7.2 g/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for ranch dressing. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB source; USDA SR Legacy values used as cross-reference. Trans fat set to -1 as not directly reported; estimated trace presence noted in clinical notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of salad dressings and high-fat condiments — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mayonnaise-based dressings
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for ranch dressing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on the analogous profile of mayonnaise-based salad dressings: very high fat content (44.5 g/100 g) suppresses gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose response; carbohydrate content is only 5.9 g/100 g. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (25 × 5.9 × 0.30) / 100 = 0.44, rounded to 0.4. This is consistent with published GI values for high-fat, low-carbohydrate condiments.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold; potassium at only 64 mg/100 g provides no meaningful offset to sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit ranch dressing; a single 30 g serving delivers approximately 243 mg sodium, representing over 10% of the 2,000 mg daily limit, and the Na:K ratio is highly unfavourable for blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high total fat (44.5 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~7.2 g/100 g) impairs insulin sensitivity; estimated trace trans fat worsens insulin resistance; negligible dietary fibre (0.0 g/100 g) provides no glucose-moderating effect.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should use ranch dressing only in very small amounts as a condiment; the high saturated fat load and absence of fibre contribute to poor insulin sensitivity, and the caloric density (430 kcal/100 g) makes portion control critical for weight management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~7.2 g/100 g and estimated trace trans fat are atherogenic; sodium at 810 mg/100 g elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure; cholesterol at 26 mg/100 g adds to dietary cholesterol load.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise ranch dressing consumption; the combination of high saturated fat, elevated sodium, and estimated trace trans fat creates a compounded cardiovascular risk profile, and heart-healthy alternatives such as olive oil and lemon vinaigrette are strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trace trans fat and saturated fat (~7.2 g/100 g, exceeding the 5 g/100 g threshold) are pro-inflammatory; sodium at 810 mg/100 g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid ranch dressing; the pro-inflammatory fatty acid profile and high sodium content are contraindicated, and herb-based dressings with olive oil and lemon are recommended as anti-inflammatory alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ranch dressing is a creamy, cold American condiment and salad dressing made from an emulsified base of mayonnaise, buttermilk, and sour cream, seasoned with garlic, onion, dill, parsley, chives, and salt. It has a rich, tangy, and herbaceous flavour with a thick, pourable texture. Originating in the United States in the 1950s, ranch dressing became one of the most popular condiments in Western cuisine and is widely used in Singapore as a dip for fried chicken, vegetable crudités, pizza, and as a salad dressing. Nutritionally, ranch dressing is very high in fat (44.5 g/100 g), predominantly from the mayonnaise and dairy base, with saturated fat estimated at approximately 7.2 g/100 g. It is very high in sodium (810 mg/100 g), low in carbohydrates (5.9 g/100 g), and contains negligible dietary fibre (0.0 g/100 g). Energy density is high at 430 kcal/100 g. Clinically, ranch dressing is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very high sodium content, unsuitable for those with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high saturated fat and absence of fibre, and unsuitable for those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory fatty acid profile. It is halal-certified in many commercial formulations. Portion control is essential; a standard serving is approximately 2 tablespoons (30 g).
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in ranch dressing?
What is ranch dressing made of?
Is ranch dressing suitable for hypertension?
Ranch dressing vs Caesar dressing nutrition

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values from HPB Singapore are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for regular ranch dressing and are retained with high confidence. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients are set to -1 due to absence in the HPB source record. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for ranch dressing. Overall confidence is medium due to the fatty acid estimation and GI analogy.
notes: 1. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid breakdown is not reported in the HPB Singapore source record for this item, and USDA SR Legacy does not provide a single authoritative split for this specific formulation. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source record for ranch dressing and detailed values are not available in the primary approved source for this specific formulation. 2. Saturated fat (7.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fatty acid profile for regular ranch dressing (FDC ID 172942) as the HPB record does not report saturated fat directly. 3. Trans fat is estimated as trace (approximately 0.1 g/100 g) based on the commercial mayonnaise base; this is documented in health context notes but the per_100g field is set to -1 as no approved source directly reports this value for this specific formulation. 4. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g as confirmed by the HPB source record; this is a naturally low-fibre emulsified condiment with no plant fibre-contributing ingredients in meaningful quantity. 5. Food group corrected from input 'Meat and alternatives / Chicken' to 'Oils, seasonings and condiments / Salad dressings and dips' as ranch dressing is a condiment, not a meat or chicken product. 6. GI is an analogous estimate of 25 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database profile for high-fat, low-carbohydrate mayonnaise-based dressings; no direct GI measurement for ranch dressing is available in the approved GI database. 7. Aliases consistency check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language.english appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. 8. FoodEx2 match type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
